blocks.content: His mom got home at seven-thirty, still in her diner apron, smelling like coffee.
He was on the porch when she got there. She went inside without stopping — fifteen minutes in the kitchen, then she came out and sat on the top step.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: How was the day.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Good. Water tower.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: The old one?

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: The old one.
She was quiet for a second.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: Be careful on that old tower.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yes ma'am.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: Mr. Whitaker still on that block?

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: He gave us Cokes.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: He did that when I was a kid too.
Kit looked at her.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You knew him then?

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: He's been on that block forever.
She was looking at the street. The evening light was doing what it had been doing all afternoon — coming in at an angle, everything it touched going gold.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: Did you get caught in the storm?

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Hardware store on Cedar.

blocks.speaker: Kit's Mom
blocks.content: Mmhm.
She said she had calls to make and went inside. The screen door closed behind her — the specific snap of it, the way it settled in the frame.
Kit stayed on the porch and watched the street.
